county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Whatcom County in 1873. Named for the San Juan Islands. The Pig War (1859-1872) between the US and Britain over island sovereignty delayed full American settlement. Friday Harbor on San Juan Island is the county seat. The county comprises over 400 islands, of which about 60 are inhabited.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Juan County's history is uniquely shaped by the Pig War boundary dispute (1859-1872) between the US and Britain, which left the islands under joint military occupation for 13 years. British and American camp records from this period are at NARA and the British National Archives — they document soldiers, settlers, and Hudson's Bay Company employees on the islands. Before 1873, check records under Whatcom County. The San Juan Island National Historical Park maintains research files on both camps. The Northwest Regional Branch of the State Archives in Bellingham holds San Juan County records. The San Juan Historical Museum in Friday Harbor has pioneer family files and maritime records. The islands' lime kilns employed immigrant workers whose records are scattered. FamilySearch has microfilmed early San Juan County vital records. Many island families had maritime connections to British Columbia — check Victoria, BC records. Island isolation preserved some records exceptionally well.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

source_profiles.source_name: San Juan County Auditor — Land Records
source_profiles.repository: San Juan County Auditor, Friday Harbor
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes island properties and tidelands claims.

source_profiles.source_name: Pig War Military Camp Records — San Juan Island
source_profiles.coverage_notes: American Camp and English Camp records from the joint military occupation during the Pig War boundary dispute.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA and British National Archives hold records. NHP has research files.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Small island population.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Well documented by local historical society.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Whatcom County in 1883. Named for the Skagit tribe. The Skagit River delta is one of Washington's most productive agricultural areas. Mount Vernon is the county seat. The Swinomish and Upper Skagit reservations are within the county. The tulip fields of the Skagit Valley are internationally known.
county_metadata.research_tips: Skagit County's fertile river delta attracted diverse agricultural settlers, and its genealogical records reflect Norwegian, Swedish, and Finnish farming communities alongside the Skagit and Swinomish tribes. The Northwest Regional Branch of the State Archives in Bellingham holds Skagit County records. Before 1883, check records under Whatcom County. The Skagit County Historical Museum in La Conner has pioneer family files and agricultural history collections. The Swinomish and Upper Skagit tribal communities are documented in BIA records at NARA Seattle. The salmon canning industry along the Skagit River employed Chinese, Japanese, and Filipino workers — cannery records are scattered but NARA Seattle has some files. The logging industry in the upper Skagit valley left company records at various repositories. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. La Conner's art colony attracted creative families whose presence is documented in local publications.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes fertile delta farmland transactions.
Marriage records from county formation. Scandinavian farming community well represented.

source_profiles.source_name: Swinomish and Upper Skagit Agency Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Swinomish and Upper Skagit tribes. Census rolls, allotment records, and fishing rights documentation.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). Shows diverse farming and fishing communities.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives NW Branch, Bellingham
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations and petitions filed by Scandinavian and other European immigrants settling the Skagit Valley.

source_profiles.source_name: Mount Vernon Argus and Skagit Valley Papers

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers covering agriculture, fishing, and community events.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Early territorial records sparse due to very small population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created as an original Washington Territory county in 1854 from Clark County. Named from a Sahaptin word meaning 'swift water,' referring to the Columbia River rapids. Washington's least populated county for most of its history. Located in the Columbia River Gorge. Mount St. Helens is in the northern part. Bonneville Dam is at the western edge.
county_metadata.research_tips: Skamania County has been one of Washington's least populated counties since its formation, making its record set very small and manageable for thorough research. The Columbia River Gorge location means many families had connections across the river to Oregon (Hood River and Multnomah counties). Bonneville Dam construction (1934-1938) brought temporary workers whose records are at the Army Corps of Engineers. The 1980 Mount St. Helens eruption devastated the northern portion of the county and is documented in USGS and Forest Service records. The Skamania County Historical Society in Stevenson has pioneer family files. County Auditor records date from the 1850s but early entries are sparse. FamilySearch has microfilmed vital records. Bridge of the Gods and Wind Mountain area hold Native American archaeological significance. The county's timber industry records at various logging companies document workers from the late 1800s forward.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records; sparse territorial-era records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Small volume due to low population.
Marriage records from territorial era. Very small volume.
Federal census from 1860. Consistently one of Washington's smallest populations.
Employment and construction records from Bonneville Dam, which brought workers to the area.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good at NARA and Corps of Engineers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents in the Columbia Gorge area.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Island County in 1861. Named for the Snohomish tribe. Everett became the county seat and a major Puget Sound industrial city. Home to the Tulalip Indian Reservation. Boeing's Everett factory (world's largest building by volume) has been a major employer since 1967.
county_metadata.research_tips: Snohomish County's Everett was built as an industrial city by eastern capitalists in the 1890s who envisioned it as the 'Pittsburgh of the West.' The Everett Massacre of 1916 (IWW labor conflict) is documented in labor history collections at the University of Washington. The Tulalip Tribes' reservation north of Marysville has records at NARA Seattle and the Tulalip tribal archives. The Northwest Regional Branch of the State Archives in Bellingham holds Snohomish County records. The Everett Public Library has city directories and local history files. Logging was the dominant industry before Boeing arrived — check Weyerhaeuser Company records (headquartered nearby) for employee information. The Snohomish County Museum of History has pioneer family files. FamilySearch has extensive Snohomish County microfilm. Monte Cristo mining district records at the Forest Service and BLM document 1890s gold and silver miners. Japanese strawberry farming communities in the Marysville area are documented in Densho collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; online search available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Online index available.
Marriage records from territorial era. Indexed through Digital Archives.
BIA records for Tulalip Reservation covering Snohomish, Snoqualmie, Skykomish, and other tribes.
Federal census from 1870. Shows lumber workers, fishermen, and later industrial workers.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives NW Branch, Bellingham
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization records covering diverse immigrant communities — Scandinavian, Japanese, Filipino, Italian mill workers.

source_profiles.source_name: Everett Herald and Snohomish County Papers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Everett newspapers including coverage of the 1916 Everett Massacre.

county_metadata.record_losses: The Great Spokane Fire of August 4, 1889 destroyed 32 blocks of downtown but courthouse records largely survived.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Walla Walla County in 1858 as Spokane County. The city and county were originally spelled 'Spokan' and 'Spokane Falls.' Spokane is the largest city in eastern Washington and the Inland Empire hub. The 1889 fire destroyed downtown shortly before statehood. Home to Gonzaga University and Fairchild Air Force Base.
county_metadata.research_tips: Spokane is the Inland Empire's largest city and its records serve as a hub for eastern Washington research. The great fire of 1889 destroyed downtown Spokane but courthouse records largely survived because the county offices were rebuilt quickly. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in nearby Cheney is the primary archival repository. The Spokane Public Library's Northwest Room has city directories from the 1890s, obituary indexes, and photograph collections. Fairchild Air Force Base (from 1942) generated military personnel records. The Spokane Tribe of Indians has a reservation northwest of the city — tribal records at NARA Seattle. Gonzaga University archives hold Catholic parish records and Jesuit mission records relevant to both settler and Native American families. The mining industry in the Coeur d'Alene district across the Idaho border employed many Spokane residents. FamilySearch has extensive Spokane County microfilm. The city's railroad junction status means records of workers from multiple railroad companies can be found here.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; online index and images

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Online index available. Survived 1889 fire.
Marriage records from territorial era. Major volume reflecting regional hub status.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives Eastern Branch, Cheney
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Large collection reflecting Spokane's role as regional center. German, Scandinavian, Italian, and other European immigrants.
Federal census from 1860. Rapid growth from 1880s. Shows railroad, mining, and agricultural workers.

source_profiles.source_name: Gonzaga University Archives — Jesuit Mission Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Jesuit records from Pacific Northwest missions including Sacred Heart Mission records documenting Native American and settler families in the Inland Empire.
Major regional newspaper for the Inland Empire. Extensive obituary, legal notice, and community coverage.

source_profiles.repository: NARA / Air Force Historical Research Agency

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early territorial records sparse.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Walla Walla County in 1863. Named after Governor Isaac Stevens, Washington Territory's first governor. Originally covered the entire northeastern portion of the territory. Colville was an important fur trade post (Fort Colville). Parent county to Ferry, Pend Oreille, and parts of other northeastern counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Stevens County was the parent county for much of northeastern Washington, so early records here may cover families who later lived in Ferry, Pend Oreille, and other counties formed from it. Fort Colville (HBC post) and Fort Colville (US Army post) are distinct — the HBC fort predates American settlement and its records are at the Hudson's Bay Company Archives in Winnipeg. The US Army's Fort Colville (1859-1882) records are at NARA. The Colville Confederated Tribes' agency headquarters is in Nespelem (Ferry County) but historical records at NARA Seattle cover Stevens County families. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Stevens County records. The Stevens County Historical Society in Colville has mining and ranching family files. Chewelah and other small towns have local historical societies with supplementary materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Stevens County vital records. Mining districts including the Old Dominion mine have claim records at BLM.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Originally covered vast northeastern WA territory.
Marriage records from territorial era. Cover broad area before child counties were formed.

source_profiles.source_name: Colville Agency Records — Stevens County Area
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for tribes in the Stevens County area including Colville, Spokane, and Kalispel peoples.

Federal land patents in northeastern Washington.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original territorial county (from Lewis under Oregon Territory)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created in 1852 under Oregon Territory. Named after Samuel Thurston, Oregon's first territorial delegate. Olympia became the territorial capital in 1853 and state capital in 1889. As the capital county, Thurston has the deepest connection to government records. The Nisqually Indian Reservation and Chehalis Reservation are partially within the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Thurston County is the seat of Washington's state government, so researchers benefit from proximity to the Washington State Archives, State Library, and other state agencies — all in Olympia. Olympia was the territorial capital from 1853, and territorial government records here predate most county records in the state. The Nisqually tribe's reservation is in the county — the Medicine Creek Treaty of 1854 was signed nearby and tribal records are at NARA Seattle. Fort Steilacoom (originally in Thurston County before Pierce County was separated) records are at NARA. The Olympia Tumwater Foundation maintains records of the early settlement. The Thurston County Auditor has deeds from the 1850s including Donation Land Claims. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. Olympia city directories from the 1880s at the state library can supplement census records. The Chehalis tribe's records at NARA Seattle also cover families in southern Thurston County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes Donation Land Claims and capital city properties.
DLCs from earliest American settlement around Puget Sound. Names wives of claimants.
Marriage records from earliest territorial period.
BIA records for Nisqually and Chehalis tribes. Medicine Creek Treaty records, census rolls, and allotment files.
Federal census from 1850 (Oregon Territory). Shows territorial government officials alongside settlers.

source_profiles.source_name: Thurston County Superior Court — Probate Records
source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives / Thurston County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files from territorial era. Include estates of early government officials and settlers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — among oldest in the state.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created as an original Washington Territory county in 1854. Named after the Wahkiakum band of the Chinook people. Washington's smallest county by both area and population. Located along the Columbia River. Cathlamet is the county seat. Puget Island is the largest river island in the Columbia.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wahkiakum County is the smallest county in Washington by area and among the smallest by population, making its genealogical records extremely manageable — a determined researcher can review nearly the entire county record set. The county's Scandinavian fishing heritage is strong, particularly among Norwegian and Finnish families who settled along the Columbia River. Cathlamet has been the county seat since formation and records have stayed in one place. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Wahkiakum County records. The Wahkiakum County Historical Society has photographs and family files. Columbia River fishing and logging defined the economy — check cannery records at NARA Seattle. Puget Island's farming community is documented in local church records. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. Cross-river connections to Oregon (Westport ferry) mean some families have records in Clatsop County, Oregon.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Very small volume makes comprehensive review practical.
Marriage records from territorial era. Very small volume.
Federal census from 1860. Shows Scandinavian fishermen and loggers.

source_profiles.source_name: Scandinavian Lutheran Church Records — Lower Columbia
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Norwegian and Finnish Lutheran congregation records from Columbia River communities.
Early DLCs along the Columbia River.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created as one of Washington Territory's original counties in 1854. Named from the Sahaptin word 'walla walla' meaning 'many waters.' Originally the largest county in the territory, parent to many eastern Washington counties. Walla Walla was the early population center of eastern Washington. Home to Whitman College, the Washington State Penitentiary, and Fort Walla Walla.
county_metadata.research_tips: Walla Walla was the population and commercial center of eastern Washington in the territorial era, and its records are the parent source for most of eastern Washington's county records. The Whitman Mission Massacre of 1847 is one of the most documented events in Pacific Northwest history — records at the Oregon Historical Society, Whitman College archives, and NARA. Fort Walla Walla military records (1856-1910) at NARA document Army personnel during the Indian Wars. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Walla Walla County records. The county's early Italian wine-growing community is documented in Catholic parish records. Whitman College's Penrose Library has extensive regional manuscripts. The Washington State Penitentiary records (from 1887) are at the state archives and document prisoners with genealogical detail. FamilySearch has extensive Walla Walla County microfilm. The county's wheat farming heritage and its role as a staging point for Idaho gold rush miners make its records valuable for tracing families headed east.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from territorial era

source_profiles.repository: Walla Walla County Auditor, Walla Walla
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Parent county to much of eastern WA.
Post returns, personnel records, and quartermaster files from the Indian Wars-era fort.
Marriage records from territorial era. Cover broad area before child counties formed.
Federal census from 1860. Shows diverse population including miners, farmers, soldiers, and Chinese merchants.

source_profiles.source_name: Whitman Mission and Catholic Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Whitman College Archives / Diocese of Baker
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records from the 1836 Whitman Mission (Protestant) and later Catholic parishes serving Italian and other immigrant families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Whitman College holds mission-era records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Prisoner records including physical descriptions, photographs, family information, and commitment papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created as an original Washington Territory county in 1854. Named from the Nooksack word meaning 'noisy water.' Bellingham (formed from merger of four towns in 1903) is the county seat. Located on the Canadian border. Home to Western Washington University and the Northwest Regional Branch of the State Archives. Parent county to San Juan and Skagit counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Whatcom County borders British Columbia, and cross-border family connections are extremely common — many families have records in both Washington and BC. Bellingham was formed from four separate towns (Whatcom, Sehome, Bellingham, and Fairhaven) in 1903, so early records may be under any of these names. The Northwest Regional Branch of the State Archives in Bellingham (at WWU) holds records for Whatcom, Island, San Juan, Skagit, and Snohomish counties — it is an essential research stop. Coal mining at Bellingham Bay and along the Nooksack River in the 1850s-1890s drew workers whose records are in mining company files. The Nooksack Tribe and Lummi Nation both have reservations in the county — tribal records at NARA Seattle. The Center for Pacific Northwest Studies at WWU has regional manuscript collections. FamilySearch has extensive Whatcom County microfilm. The salmon canning industry at Bellingham Bay employed Chinese, Japanese, and Filipino workers. Canadian border-crossing records at NARA Seattle track movement between BC and Washington.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes coal mine properties and tidelands.

source_profiles.repository: WA Digital Archives / WA State Archives NW Branch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from territorial era. Cover families from four pre-merger Bellingham towns.
BIA records for Lummi Nation and Nooksack Tribe. Census rolls, allotment records, and fishing rights documentation.

source_profiles.source_name: Canadian Border Crossing Records — Whatcom County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of crossings at Blaine, Sumas, and other Whatcom County border points. Track movement between BC and WA.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good at NARA Seattle and indexed on Ancestry.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1860. Shows coal miners, fishermen, and Canadian-born settlers.

source_profiles.source_name: Bellingham Herald and Whatcom County Papers
source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Center for PNW Studies (WWU)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Bellingham and predecessor town newspapers. Coal mining, fishing, and border trade coverage.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Stevens County in 1871. Named after Marcus Whitman, missionary killed in the 1847 Whitman Massacre. Located in the Palouse hills of southeastern Washington. Colfax is the county seat. Home to Washington State University in Pullman. Parent county to Adams County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Whitman County's Pullman is home to Washington State University, whose manuscripts, archives, and special collections hold important eastern Washington research materials including agricultural records, oral histories, and German-Russian community documentation. Colfax, the county seat, has continuous records from 1871. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Whitman County records. The Palouse region attracted wheat farmers from the Midwest and German-Russian settlers whose naturalization records are at the state archives. The county's rolling Palouse hills produced some of the highest wheat yields in the nation. WSU's Plateau Peoples' Web Portal documents Native American history. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Whitman County vital records. The county was parent to Adams County, so pre-1883 Adams County records are here. Pullman's railroad depot connected to the Oregon Railroad and Navigation Company, and its records document passengers and freight.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes Palouse wheat land transactions.
Marriage records from county formation.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives Eastern Branch, Cheney
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations and petitions from German-Russian and Scandinavian wheat farming immigrants.
Federal census from 1880. Shows wheat farming families in the Palouse.
Federal homestead patents in the prime Palouse wheat land.
Local newspapers covering Palouse farming community, WSU campus, and railroad news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; WSU Libraries hold microfilm.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Walla Walla County in 1865. Named after the Yakama people (the county uses the older spelling 'Yakima'). The Yakama Indian Reservation covers a large portion of the county. Yakima is the county seat, moved from Yakima City (now Union Gap) in 1884 when the Northern Pacific Railroad bypassed the original town. Major agricultural center for hops, apples, and other crops.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yakima County has one of the most complex genealogical landscapes in Washington due to the interplay of the Yakama Nation, agricultural immigration, and multiple ethnic communities. The Yakama Indian Reservation covers much of the county and tribal records at NARA Seattle and the Yakama Nation Cultural Center are essential for Native American research. The county's agricultural industry attracted successive waves of workers — Chinese railroad laborers in the 1880s, Japanese hop pickers and farmers in the early 1900s (White River Valley), Filipino asparagus cutters, and Mexican farmworkers from the 1940s onward. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Yakima County records. The Yakima Valley Museum has extensive regional history collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed Yakima County vital records. The county seat moved from Yakima City (Union Gap) to North Yakima (now Yakima) in 1884 — check records under both names. Japanese-American farming families interned during WWII are documented in WRA records at NARA and Densho.org.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes irrigation district land and orchard properties.

source_profiles.repository: NARA Seattle / Yakama Nation Cultural Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Yakama Nation including census rolls, allotment records, treaty documents, and agency correspondence. The Yakama Reservation is one of the largest in Washington.
Marriage records from territorial era. Reflect diverse agricultural community.

source_profiles.source_name: Japanese-American Farming Community Records — Yakima Valley
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of Japanese farming families in the Yakima Valley including WWII internment and resettlement records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Densho provides free digital access. NARA holds WRA records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1870. Shows diverse agricultural workforce. Reservation enumerated separately.
Parish records in English and Spanish serving Yakima Valley communities including Mexican farmworker families.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major regional newspaper covering agricultural industry, irrigation development, and diverse community news.
